extracted_evidence: The actual project period was longer than planned (ratio against the plan: 124%)
it was proportional to the increase in the length of the targeted roads, i.e., outputs, from planned 300km (approximate) at the appraisal to actual 376.68km (ratio against the plan: 126%)

Contributing factors behind the longer project period other than the increase in the outputs included, a delay in mobilizing the Special Assistance for Project Implementation by JICA and the interruptions of part of the construction works by the flooding
Nevertheless, a delay exceeding the schedule that was revised due to the increase in the outputs was avoided by controlling the implementation schedule.
response.modality_check: The text presents reported facts about the project's completion timeline, noting an extended duration compared to the original plan, but explaining it through scope increases and subsequent schedule revisions.
response.scope_recovery_analysis: Although the project period was 124% of the original plan, this was proportional to a 126% increase in outputs (road length). The schedule was officially revised to accommodate this scope change. While there were minor delays due to mobilization and flooding, the text explicitly states that a delay exceeding the revised schedule was avoided.
response.commitment_rationale: The timeline extension was directly tied to an approved scope increase (more roads built). Because the project met the revised schedule and explicitly avoided delays beyond this approved revision, it falls under 'No Delay' as the timeline change was an approved scope extension rather than an unresolved slippage.
response.label: No Delay

extracted_evidence: At the time of appraisal, the project period was estimated to be from the signing of the loan agreement in September 2001 up to October 2005 (4 years and two months) (50 months).
When examined at the time of the ex-post evaluation, the construction was completed in June 2012 (10 years and 10 months) (130 months).
The project period was 260% longer than initially planned.
The executing agency spent longer than expected selecting consultants because of the approval process within the Moroccan government.
Moreover, the construction was thought to be delayed because the additional financing required the construction cost to be estimated again, and there was also a project optimization study.
In light of the above, the project period was significantly longer than planned.
response.modality_check: The extracted quotes are reported facts from an ex-post evaluation detailing the actual timeline and the reasons for the extended duration, not hypothetical risks or policies.
response.scope_recovery_analysis: The project experienced a massive, unrecovered delay, taking 130 months instead of the planned 50 months. While some of the delay was tied to additional financing and optimization studies, it was heavily compounded by administrative delays, communication issues, and personnel problems. The delay was never recovered.
response.commitment_rationale: The text explicitly confirms a severe schedule slippage, stating the project took 260% longer than planned and detailing the specific causes of the delay. There is no ambiguity, making 'Delay' the definitive classification.
response.label: Delay

extracted_evidence: The project period was significantly longer than the initial plan due to delays in procedures, design and construction.

extracted_evidence: Implementation was delayed because of (i) a change in the scope of works, i.e., from 4 lanes to 6 lanes; (ii) a change in the design of bridge piles due to seismic design provisions; and (iii) uncertain geotechnical conditions encountered during the piling works for one bridge.
